items.q: Is the amount of water on Earth decreasing as we use it?
items.correct: No — the global total is essentially fixed and cycles between stores; the issue is where water is, in what form, and how fast it transfers, not the planet running dry.
items.random_wrong: Most of Earth's water escapes to space each year.
items.why: Seeing water as a closed cycle with uneven stores and fluxes reframes scarcity correctly.

items.q: Does carbon stay in the place where it is emitted?
items.correct: No — carbon moves between the atmosphere, oceans, biosphere and soils through fluxes such as photosynthesis, respiration, ocean uptake and combustion; sinks absorb a large share of human emissions.
items.misconception: CO2 released by a country stays in the air above that country.
items.random_wrong: Carbon emitted at night falls back to the ground by morning.
items.why: Treating carbon as locally fixed prevents understanding of sinks, fluxes and global carbon budgets.

items.q: Are the water and carbon cycles independent of each other?
items.correct: No — they interlink: vegetation transpires water while storing carbon, oceans absorb both heat and CO2, and carbon-driven climate change alters precipitation and evaporation.
items.misconception: The two cycles can be studied as completely separate systems.
items.random_wrong: Carbon and water never occur in the same places.
items.why: The specification explicitly requires understanding how the two cycles interrelate.

items.q: Oceans hold what percentage of global water?
items.why: Correct answer: 97 percent. “67 percent” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Atmospheric residence time of water is closest to:
items.why: It's 9 days. “9 hours” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximate ocean residence time in years:
items.why: Exact answer: 3200. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
